answers.field_label: Coach Supervision Related Training Qualifications And Certifications Including The Date Duration And Level
answers.value:
1) Professional Coach Certification (PCC) Program – Columbia University Coaching Certification Program
   - Institution: Columbia University, Teachers College (New York, NY)
   - Dates: 2012-01-15 to 2012-12-10
   - Duration: Approx. 9 months, blended format (8 intensive in-person days + monthly virtual modules)
   - Level: ICF-accredited coach training, 180 coach-specific training hours, aligned to ICF PCC-level competencies.
   - Focus: Foundational and intermediate coaching skills, coaching ethics, coaching methodology (cognitive-behavioral, solution-focused, and systems thinking approaches), supervised practice with observed coaching sessions and feedback.

2) Advanced Certificate in Executive & Leadership Coaching
   - Institution: Hudson Institute of Coaching (Santa Barbara, CA)
   - Dates: 2014-03-05 to 2014-11-20
   - Duration: 8-month program (4 x 3-day in-person intensives + group supervision calls)
   - Level: Advanced practitioner level, focused on executive and organizational leadership coaching.
   - Focus: Working with senior leaders and teams, navigating organizational politics, coaching for strategic thinking, and using assessments (360 feedback, Hogan, EQ-i 2.0). Included 24 hours of mentor coaching and supervision on recorded client sessions.

3) Diploma in Coaching Supervision
   - Institution: Oxford Brookes University – International Centre for Coaching & Mentoring Studies (Oxford, UK – distance and residential)
   - Dates: 2018-02-10 to 2019-02-02
   - Duration: 12 months, part-time (three 3-day residential modules + ongoing triad practice and supervision)
   - Level: Postgraduate diploma-level program (Level 7 equivalent), specialized in coaching supervision.
   - Focus: Reflective practice, systemic and relational supervision models, ethics in supervision, working with parallel process, group supervision facilitation, and contracting for supervision. Required a supervised log of 40+ hours of actual supervision practice.

4) EMCC Senior Practitioner (Coaching) & Practitioner (Supervision) Accreditation
   - Accrediting Body: European Mentoring & Coaching Council (EMCC)
   - Dates: Coaching accreditation awarded 2016-06-15; Supervision accreditation awarded 2020-09-28
   - Duration: Portfolio-based accreditation with rigorous assessment of practice hours and CPD.
   - Level: Senior Practitioner (Coaching) – demonstrating advanced level of coaching practice; Practitioner (Supervision) – established level in supervision practice.
   - Focus: Demonstrated evidence of 750+ coaching hours with clients, sustained reflective practice, regular supervision, and ongoing CPD. For supervision, documented 70+ hours of supervision practice and reflective essays on supervision philosophy and approach.

5) ICF Professional Certified Coach (PCC)
   - Accrediting Body: International Coaching Federation
   - Date Awarded: 2017-04-12 (renewed 2020-04-10 and 2023-04-08)
   - Level: PCC – mid-advanced level credential.
   - Requirements Met: Over 750 hours of paid coaching, 125+ hours of coach-specific training, 10+ hours of mentor coaching, performance evaluation via recorded coaching sessions, and ongoing CCE hours for renewal.

6) Certificate in Team Coaching and Systems Work
   - Institution: Team Coaching International (virtual with in-person intensive in San Francisco, CA)
   - Dates: 2019-05-01 to 2019-10-15
   - Duration: 6-month program (two 3-day intensives + virtual labs and supervision)
   - Level: Practitioner level certification in team coaching.
   - Focus: Systems-psychodynamic and relationship systems work with intact teams, contracting with sponsors, diagnostic tools for team effectiveness, and live supervised team-coaching practice with feedback from faculty.

7) Ongoing CPD and Workshops (selection)
   - 2020-2024: Average 25–35 hours per year of CPD focused on ethics in coaching supervision, trauma-informed coaching, DEI in coaching and supervision, and advanced reflective practice methods (journaling, peer-supervision groups). Examples include:
     • "Ethics and Power in Coaching Supervision" – 2-day online intensive with Prof. Tatiana Bachkirova (2021-03-11 to 2021-03-12).
     • "Coaching Across Cultures" – 3-day workshop with Philippe Rosinski (2022-06-06 to 2022-06-08).
     • Monthly peer-supervision circle since 2020-01 (2 hours/month) with a mixed group of executive coaches and supervisors across the US and UK.

Collectively, these trainings and certifications have shaped a supervision approach that is relational, reflective, and systemic. In practice, I often combine Hawkins & Shohet’s 7-Eyed Model, a strengths-based lens from positive psychology, and a strong emphasis on ethics and boundaries. I maintain an active reflective journal, review recordings of both my coaching and supervision sessions (with client consent), and bring my own dilemmas to supervision at least once a month to ensure I continue to grow and stay grounded in best practice.

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach A Coach
answers.value:
I began practicing as an individual coach in mid-2012, shortly after starting the Columbia University coaching certification program. Counting from 2012-06 to the present (2026-01), I have been coaching for approximately 13.5 years. In terms of intensity:
- 2012–2014: Part-time (5–10 client hours per month) while transitioning from an internal HR leadership role.
- 2015–2018: Full-time independent executive coach (15–25 client hours per week), primarily working with mid-level and senior leaders in technology and healthcare organizations.
- 2019–present: Combination of coaching and supervision, averaging 12–18 one-to-one coaching hours per week.
Over this period, I have accumulated just over 1,600 hours of one-to-one coaching with approximately 140 individual clients, ranging from first-time managers to C-suite executives. The majority of my work is executive and leadership development coaching, with some career transition and resilience-focused engagements.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach Team Coach
answers.value:
I started formal team coaching in 2016 as part of an internal engagement with a product leadership team in a mid-sized software company. Initially, I approached it more like a series of facilitated workshops, but from 2019 onward (after completing the Team Coaching International certification), my practice became more structured and clearly positioned as team coaching.

If I count from my first structured, explicitly contracted team coaching engagement in 2016-09 to the present (2026-01), I have been practicing as a team coach for a little over 9 years. In practical terms:
- 2016–2018: 1–2 team engagements per year (typically 6–9 month engagements with quarterly in-person sessions).
- 2019–2021: 3–4 team engagements per year, often with distributed/remote teams in tech and professional services.
- 2022–present: 2–3 larger, more complex team coaching engagements per year, each running 9–12 months.

Cumulatively, I have worked with approximately 25 distinct leadership and project teams. My focus has been on leadership alignment, cross-functional collaboration, psychological safety, and supporting newly formed leadership teams after mergers or reorganizations.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach Coach Supervisor
answers.value:
I began offering coaching supervision on a semi-formal basis in late 2018 while enrolled in the Oxford Brookes Coaching Supervision Diploma, initially working with a small group of three volunteer coaches in exchange for permission to use anonymized material for my course assignments. After completing the diploma in early 2019 and obtaining my EMCC Supervision accreditation in 2020, I transitioned to offering paid supervision.

From 2018-10 to the present (2026-01), I have been practicing as a coach supervisor for a little over 7 years. The progression has been:
- 2018-10 to 2019-02: Practicum phase, 2–3 supervisees, individual and small-group sessions, under faculty supervision.
- 2019–2021: Building a supervision practice alongside coaching, averaging 6–8 regular supervisees (mix of one-to-one and small groups), about 6–8 supervision hours per month.
- 2022–present: More established supervision portfolio, typically 10–14 regular supervisees plus ad hoc group supervision offerings, averaging 10–14 supervision hours per month.

To date, I have logged approximately 320 hours of formal coaching supervision (both individual and group), supporting internal coaches, independent executive coaches, and a small number of team coaches. My supervision work is heavily influenced by the 7-Eyed Model, person-centered approaches, and a strong emphasis on ethical reflection and the well-being of both coach and client system.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach Other
answers.value:
In addition to individual and team coaching and formal supervision, I have been active in several related roles:

1) Internal Mentor Coach (prior to going fully independent)
   - Timeframe: 2010-01 to 2014-06
   - Organization: Northwest Health Systems (Portland, OR)
   - Role & Duration: Approximately 4.5 years informally mentoring and coaching high-potential HR business partners and early-career managers while I was serving as Director of Talent Development. I typically carried 3–5 internal mentees at a time, meeting monthly for 60–90 minutes. While this was not always labeled "coaching" at the time, the work was strongly coaching-oriented (goal setting, reflective questioning, stakeholder feedback).

2) Mentor Coach for ICF Credentialing Candidates
   - Timeframe: 2017-06 to present (2026-01)
   - Duration: Approximately 8.5 years.
   - Role: Providing mentor coaching to other coaches pursuing ICF ACC or PCC credentials. This involves listening to recorded coaching sessions, providing structured feedback linked to ICF core competencies, and supporting coaches in deepening their self-awareness and presence. I generally work with 6–10 mentees per year in this capacity, often in 3- or 6-month packages.

3) Facilitator and Trainer in Coaching Skills for Leaders
   - Timeframe: 2015-03 to present (2026-01)
   - Duration: Roughly 10.75 years.
   - Role: Designing and delivering coaching-skills workshops for line managers and senior leaders (typically 1–3 day programs) to help create more coaching-oriented cultures within organizations. This is not "coaching" in the strict sense, but it relies heavily on my coaching expertise and often leads to short, follow-up coaching or supervision-style support as participants begin applying the skills.

Across these related roles, I have probably accumulated an additional 400–500 hours of coaching-adjacent practice (mentoring, feedback on recorded sessions, skills training, and reflective debriefs). These experiences give me a broader systemic view of how coaching is embedded in organizations and inform the way I support both coaches and leaders who are trying to adopt a coaching mindset in their day-to-day work.
